sections.paragraph:
πρόγλωσσος, ον (Ptolem., Apotel. 3, 14, 31 Boll-B.; Ps.-Polemon Physiogn. 37 Förster et al.) pert. to ready use of the tongue, hasty of tongue, talkative (Pythag., Ep. 12, 2 of the ideal nurse, who is to set an example of composure by not being garrulous) B 19:8. As suggested by the clause that follows: παγὶς γὰρ τὸ στόμα θανάτου, perh. with implication of being gossipy.

Ptolem Ptolem , math., II a.d.—List 5

Boll Boll = FB., Aus der Offenb. Johannis—List 6

  1. B. = CDBuck, A Dictionary of Selected Synonyms—List 6

Ps. Ps. = pseudo, pseudonymous

Physiogn. Physiogn. = Scriptores Physiognomici, various dates—List 5

et al. et al. = et alii (and others)

pert. pert. = pertaining (to)

Pythag Pythag = letters attributed to the phil. Pythagoras (VI–V b.c.); s. Ep.; date uncertain—List 5

Ep.

Ep. = Epistola/Epistula, when applied to letters mostly pseudonymous; various dates—List 5

  1. = epistle

B B = Barnabas (the Letter of), II a.d., except in series of uncial witnesses, in which case B refers to Codex Vaticanus (s. also Vat.). When the abbrv. B would ambiguous, Vat. is used for the codex.—List 1

perh. perh. = perhaps

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 866
